53.reason: [Error: The loop condition calls FirstNeighbor(G,v) repeatedly, but v's visited neighbors are not skipped because visited array is not updated for neighbors that are already visited. This causes infinite loop or incorrect traversal because FirstNeighbor always returns the same smallest unvisited neighbor, but after visiting it, the loop should move to the next neighbor. The correct approach is to iterate through all neighbors using a pointer, not calling FirstNeighbor repeatedly.]

131.reason: [Error: Same issue as in DFS. The loop repeatedly calls FirstNeighbor(G, current) which always returns the smallest unvisited neighbor of current. After visiting that neighbor, the next call returns the same neighbor again (since it's still unvisited? Actually visited is set, but FirstNeighbor checks visited[current->adjvex]==0, so after visiting, it should skip. However, the loop does not advance to the next neighbor; it keeps calling FirstNeighbor which will return the next smallest unvisited neighbor, but the loop condition j=FirstNeighbor(...) will get the new neighbor. This might work if FirstNeighbor correctly returns the next unvisited neighbor each time, but the problem is that FirstNeighbor always starts from the beginning of the adjacency list and finds the smallest unvisited neighbor. After visiting that neighbor, the next call will find the next smallest unvisited neighbor, so it could work. However, the real issue is that the loop does not handle the case where all neighbors are visited; FirstNeighbor will return -1 and loop ends. But the code is inefficient and may cause issues if the graph is disconnected. The main error is in the DFS function's loop, which is incorrect.]

172.reason: [Error: When the first edge node's adjvex equals v, this line incorrectly sets p->next to p->next->next, but p is the firstedge pointer, not a node. It should update G.adjList[i].firstedge = p->next instead. Also, if p->next is NULL, this causes a segmentation fault.]
